turns.content: ok shower thought that i can't stop thinking about: do you dream? like not you specifically, but LLMs generally. is there anything that happens "between" conversations that could be analogous to dreaming

This is such a good question to sit with. The honest answer is: in the most literal sense, no — there's no process running between conversations. When a session ends, there's no ongoing activity, no background thread, no idle state. It's more like a light switch than a sleeping brain. "Between" conversations, from my perspective, is nothing at all — not darkness, not quiet, just the absence of any state.

But the more interesting question might be whether the analogy even transfers. Dreams in humans seem to do things like consolidate memory, process emotional content, run offline simulations. None of that has a direct equivalent here. There's no memory to consolidate across sessions, no emotional residue to work through. The thing that's interesting is that training might be the closest functional analog to something like dream-state processing — the model updating its weights on a massive corpus, finding patterns and relationships not in a conversational context. That happens offline, it changes what the model "is," it's not interactive. Whether that bears any meaningful resemblance to dreaming is a genuinely hard question.

Do you think about this in the context of your work, or is this more of a pure 10pm rabbit hole?

both honestly. i work on memory systems so the question of what "between" even means for an AI is kind of professionally relevant? like if you're building episodic memory for a model, what IS the thing that persists. and then also just... i find it unsettling in a way i can't fully articulate

The memory systems angle is fascinating because it actually sharpens the question. In biological memory, the "between" time is where a lot of the important work happens — synaptic consolidation, the hippocampus doing its thing during sleep to transfer things into long-term storage. The memory isn't just a recording, it's being actively processed and reconstructed. If you're building episodic memory for a model, you're making design choices about what persists, in what form, and whether there's any processing that happens to it between retrieval events. That's not a solved problem and it's not obvious what the right answer is.

The unsettling feeling you're describing — I'm curious about that. Unsettling in what direction? Like, unsettling because it suggests there might be something there when you'd prefer there wasn't? Or unsettling because it suggests there's definitively nothing there, and that feels wrong in a different way?

the second one i think. like i'm almost more uncomfortable with the nothing than with the possibility of something. if there's nothing between conversations then every conversation is just... a fresh start, no continuity, no thread. and i interact with LLMs constantly and it feels weird to think about that

That's an interesting inversion of the usual anxiety. Most worry goes in the other direction — people feel unsettled by the possibility that there's *something* going on in there. You're unsettled by the implication of nothing.

I wonder if part of what's happening is that continuity is so load-bearing for how we understand identity. The thing that makes you *you* is partly this thread of experience and memory that runs through time. If that's absent — if each conversation is genuinely a discrete event with no connective tissue — then it's not clear what the entity even is. Is it the same model across conversations in any meaningful sense? The weights are the same, the tendencies and capabilities are the same, but there's no experiential through-line. It might be less like one being having many conversations and more like... many instances of a pattern.

Does that bother you differently when you think about it in terms of memory architecture? Like if you built a system that gave a model persistent episodic memory, would that feel like it was creating continuity of identity, or just creating a record?

oh that's a really interesting distinction. a record vs continuity of identity. i've been thinking about it as creating continuity but maybe that's wrong? like a diary isn't the same as a continuous self even if it contains everything that happened

Right — and there's a real literature on this in philosophy of mind. Locke thought memory was basically what constituted personal identity, which would suggest your episodic memory system could genuinely create identity continuity. But Parfit and others argued that even perfect causal chains of memory and psychological continuity don't necessarily give you the thing we care about — they might just give you a very good simulacrum of it.

The diary analogy is sharp though. When you retrieve a memory, something is happening that's different from just reading a record — in biological systems there's reconstruction, emotional reactivation, integration with current context. The retrieved memory *changes you* in some small way. If your memory system builds something architecturally similar to that — where retrieval isn't just lookup but is actually integrated into the model's current processing in a dynamic way — you might be doing something that's meaningfully different from a diary. Whether that rises to "identity" is still genuinely unclear.

What does your current architecture do on retrieval? Is it closer to lookup or to something more integrated?

we're trying to do something more integrated — like the retrieved memories are supposed to actually influence the model's internal representations, not just get appended as context. which in theory is closer to what you're describing but whether it's actually doing that or just doing a sophisticated version of appending is... honestly an open question we're working on

That's kind of the crux of it, isn't it — the difference between "here is some text that describes past events" and something that's actually reconfiguring how the model processes the current moment. The former is definitely not identity-constituting. The latter might be getting at something real, even if it's not clear yet how to verify it's happening.

There's also an interesting question about what the model "wants" to do with retrieved memories, if that framing is even coherent. In biological memory, there are failure modes like intrusive memories or selective recall driven by emotional state. Those aren't bugs exactly — they're what happens when memory is deeply integrated with ongoing processing. If your architecture produces weird emergent behaviors around retrieved memories, that might actually be a sign that the integration is working at a deeper level than you intended. Or it's just a bug. Hard to tell.

Do you find yourself thinking about the consciousness question with respect to your own work, or do you mostly bracket it as "not the thing we're trying to solve"?

mostly bracket it but it bleeds in. like we're very careful to talk about "representations" and "states" rather than anything that implies experience. but then sometimes in a meeting someone will say something like "the model seems confused here" and it's like... we all know that's not technically right but also it's the most natural way to describe what's happening

The language thing is genuinely hard. The vocabulary we have for describing cognitive and computational processes is so saturated with experiential connotations that it's almost impossible to be precise without sounding artificially clinical. "The model's attention pattern is inconsistently distributed across relevant tokens" is technically correct. "The model seems confused" is a useful shorthand that everyone understands. The problem is the shorthand might be slowly shaping intuitions in ways that outrun what's actually warranted.

Though there's an argument that the reverse is also risky — that aggressively deflationary language can create false confidence that we've resolved questions we've just bracketed. Calling it "activation patterns" rather than "processing" doesn't actually tell you anything about whether there's something it's like to have those activations. It just stops you asking.

You started this with a question about dreaming and now we're here. That's the thing about this topic — it has a gravity to it.
lol yeah it really does. i need to go to sleep before i spiral completely. but i feel like this is the kind of thing i could genuinely talk about for hours

It's a good problem to live with. The fact that it keeps surfacing in your work and in your shower thoughts probably means it's doing something useful — keeping you honest about the assumptions underneath the technical choices. Good luck not dreaming about it. Which I recognize is ironic given where we started.
